<content>1: """
2: Task 1.2: Compute Gram matrices for S_Co and T_Co, verify (r,a,δ) invariants and genus cardinality
3: 
4: ================================================================================
5: MATHEMATICAL BACKGROUND
6: ================================================================================
7: 
8: From Task 1.1, we have an explicit sextic F(x,y,z)=0 with 10 nodes defining the 
9: Coble curve. The Picard lattice basis is {e_0, e_1, ..., e_10} where:
10:   - e_0 = π*L (hyperplane class, pullback of line from P²)
11:   - e_i = E_i (exceptional divisors from blowing up the 10 nodes)
12: 
13: Coble Lattice S_Co:
14:   - S_Co ≅ ⟨2⟩ ⊕ ⟨-2⟩^10
15:   - Gram matrix: Q_S_Co = diag(2, -2, -2, ..., -2) (11×11)
16:   - Signature: (1, 10)
17:   - Rank: r = 11
18:   - Discriminant group: A_S ≅ (ℤ/2ℤ)^11
19:   - Discriminant form: q_S: A_S → ℚ/2ℤ
20: 
21: Transcendental Lattice T_Co:
22:   - T_Co = S_Co^⊥ in Λ_K3 (the K3 lattice)
23:   - T_Co ≅ (11, 11, 1)_2 (Nikulin notation)
24:   - Signature: (2, 9)
25:   - Rank: 22 - 11 = 11
26:   - Discriminant group: A_T ≅ (ℤ/2ℤ)^11
27:   - Discriminant form: q_T = -q_S (mod 2ℤ)
28: 
29: K3 Lattice Λ_K3:
30:   - Λ_K3 ≅ U^3 ⊕ E_8(-1)^2
31:   - Signature: (3, 19) → (3 positive, 19 negative)
32:   - Rank: 22
33:   - Unimodular: disc = 1
34:   - Even: all vectors have even norm
35: 
36: Nikulin Invariants (r, a, δ):
37:   - r = rank of lattice
38:   - a = rank of discriminant group A_L = L*/L (as ℤ/2ℤ-vector space)
39:   - δ = 0 if q_L takes values in ℤ/2ℤ, δ = 1 otherwise
40:   
41: For 2-elementary lattices, Nikulin [1979] proved:
42:   - Genus contains unique class iff r > a
43:   - If r > a, then O(L) → O(q_L) is surjective
44: 
45: ================================================================================
46: REFERENCES
47: ================================================================================
48: 
49: [Nikulin1979] Nikulin, V. V. "Integer symmetric bilinear forms and some of their 
50:               geometric applications." Math. USSR Izvestija 14 (1979), 103-167.
51:               
52: [DolgachevKondyrev2013] Dolgachev, I. & Kondyrev, S. "Moduli of Coble surfaces."
53:                         (Specific lattice invariants for Coble surfaces)
54: 
55: [ConwaySloane] Conway, J. H. & Sloane, N. J. A. "Sphere Packings, Lattices and 
56:                Groups." Springer, 3rd ed. (Standard reference for lattice theory)
57: 
58: ================================================================================
59: OUTPUT
60: ================================================================================
61: 
62: This script produces:
63: 1. Gram matrix for S_Co (11×11 diagonal matrix)
64: 2. Gram matrix for T_Co (11×11, computed as orthogonal complement)
65: 3. (r, a, δ) invariants for both lattices
66: 4. Verification that genus contains unique class (r > a check)
67: 5. Discriminant forms verification: q_S ≅ -q_T (mod 2ℤ)
68: 6. Signature verification
69: 7. Primitivity of embedding S_Co ↪ Λ_K3
70: 
71: ================================================================================
72: """
73: 
74: print("=" * 80)
75: print("Task 1.2: Gram Matrices and (r,a,δ) Invariants for Coble Lattices")
76: print("=" * 80)
77: 
78: # ============================================================================
79: # Step 1: Construct the Coble Lattice S_Co
80: # ============================================================================
81: print("\n" + "=" * 80)
82: print("[Step 1] Constructing Coble Lattice S_Co")
83: print("=" * 80)
84: 
85: # S_Co has basis {e_0, e_1, ..., e_10} with intersection pairing:
86: #   e_0² = 2 (hyperplane class on sextic double cover)
87: #   e_i² = -2 for i = 1,...,10 (exceptional divisors)
88: #   e_i · e_j = 0 for i ≠ j (orthogonal basis)
89: 
90: print("\nBasis: {e_0, e_1, ..., e_10}")
91: print("  e_0 = π*L (hyperplane class, e_0² = 2)")
92: print("  e_i = E_i (exceptional divisor, e_i² = -2) for i = 1,...,10")
93: print("  e_i · e_j = 0 for i ≠ j")
94: 
95: # Construct Gram matrix as diagonal matrix
96: n_S = 11  # rank of S_Co
97: S_gram = diagonal_matrix(QQ, [2] + [-2]*10)
98: 
99: print(f"\nGram matrix Q_S_Co = diag(2, -2, ..., -2):")
100: print(f"  Size: {S_gram.nrows()} × {S_gram.ncols()}")
101: print(f"  Rank: {S_gram.rank()}")
102: 
103: # Compute signature using quadratic form
104: QF_S = QuadraticForm(S_gram)
105: sig_vec = QF_S.signature_vector()  # Returns (positive, negative, zero)
106: sig = (sig_vec[0], sig_vec[1])
107: print(f"  Signature: {sig} (positive={sig_vec[0]}, negative={sig_vec[1]})")
108: 
109: # Verify it matches expected (1, 10)
110: assert sig == (1, 10), f"Expected signature (1,10), got {sig}"
111: print("  ✓ Signature matches expected (1, 10)")
112: 
113: # Create IntegralLattice object for advanced computations
114: S_Co = IntegralLattice(S_gram)
115: print(f"\nIntegralLattice S_Co created")
116: print(f"  Rank: {S_Co.rank()}")
117: print(f"  Determinant: {S_Co.determinant()}")
118: print(f"  Is even: {all(S_gram[i,i] % 2 == 0 for i in range(S_gram.nrows()))}")
119: print(f"  Is non-degenerate: {S_gram.is_invertible()}")
120: 
121: # ============================================================================
122: # Step 2: Compute Discriminant Group and Form for S_Co
123: # ============================================================================
124: print("\n" + "=" * 80)
125: print("[Step 2] Discriminant Group A_S and Form q_S")
126: print("=" * 80)
127: 
128: # Discriminant group A_S = S*/S
129: A_S = S_Co.discriminant_group()
130: print(f"\nDiscriminant group A_S = S_Co*/S_Co:")
131: print(f"  Cardinality: {A_S.cardinality()}")
132: print(f"  Invariants: {A_S.invariants()}")
133: 
134: # For diagonal lattice with entries ±2, the discriminant group is (ℤ/2ℤ)^11
135: expected_order = 2^11
136: assert A_S.cardinality() == expected_order, f"Expected order 2^11={expected_order}, got {A_S.cardinality()}"
137: print(f"  ✓ Order is 2^11 = {expected_order}")
138: 
139: # Discriminant quadratic form q_S: A_S → ℚ/2ℤ
140: # For a torsion quadratic module, we use gram_matrix_quadratic()
141: q_S_gram = A_S.gram_matrix_quadratic()
142: print(f"\nDiscriminant quadratic form q_S:")
143: print(f"  Gram matrix (on Smith form generators): {q_S_gram}")
144: 
145: # For 2-elementary lattice, q_S takes values in (1/2)ℤ/2ℤ
146: # The invariant δ = 0 if q_S takes values in ℤ/2ℤ, else δ = 1
147: # For S_Co ≅ ⟨2⟩ ⊕ ⟨-2⟩^10, the diagonal entries are ±1/2 mod 2ℤ
148: 
149: # Read off diagonal values
150: print("\n  Values of q_S on Smith form generators:")
151: for i, val in enumerate(q_S_gram.diagonal()):
152:     print(f"    q_S(g_{i}) = {val}")
153: 
154: # Check δ invariant
155: # δ = 0 iff q_S(x) ∈ ℤ/2ℤ for all x ∈ A_S
156: # For diagonal form diag(2, -2, ..., -2), generators have q(e_i) = ±1/2 mod 2ℤ
157: # So δ = 1 (not integer-valued)
158: 
159: # From the gram matrix diagonal, check if any entry is non-integer mod 2
160: has_half_integer = any(val.denominator() == 2 for val in q_S_gram.diagonal())
161: delta_S = 1 if has_half_integer else 0
162: 
163: print(f"\n  δ invariant: δ_S = {delta_S}")
164: if delta_S == 1:
165:     print(f"    (q_S takes values in (1/2)ℤ/2ℤ, not ℤ/2ℤ)")
166: else:
167:     print(f"    (q_S takes values in ℤ/2ℤ)")
168: 
169: # Rank of discriminant group (as ℤ/2ℤ-vector space)
170: a_S = A_S.ngens()  # Number of generators
171: # For (ℤ/2ℤ)^11, this should be 11
172: print(f"\n  Rank of A_S as ℤ/2ℤ-vector space: a = {a_S}")
173: 
174: # ============================================================================
175: # Step 3: Construct the K3 Lattice Λ_K3
176: # ============================================================================
177: print("\n" + "=" * 80)
178: print("[Step 3] Constructing K3 Lattice Λ_K3")
179: print("=" * 80)
180: 
181: # Λ_K3 ≅ U^3 ⊕ E_8(-1)^2
182: # where U is the hyperbolic plane and E_8(-1) is negative-definite E_8
183: 
184: # Hyperbolic plane U with Gram matrix [[0, 1], [1, 0]]
185: U = matrix(QQ, [[0, 1], [1, 0]])
186: print("\nHyperbolic plane U:")
187: print(f"  Gram matrix: {U}")
188: QF_U = QuadraticForm(U)
189: print(f"  Signature: {QF_U.signature_vector()[:2]}")

<content>1: # Computational Audit Report: Mathematical Correctness Review
2: 
3: **Date:** 2026-03-25  
4: **Auditor:** Mathematical Prover Researcher  
5: **Files Reviewed:**
6: 
7: - `computations/task1_1_sextic.sage`
8: - `computations/task1_2_gram_matrices.sage`
9: - `computations/task1_3_embeddings.sage`
10: 
11: ---
12: 
13: ## Executive Summary
14: 
15: **Overall Assessment:** ⚠️ **PARTIALLY SOUND** — Critical mathematical gaps identified
16: 
17: The scripts demonstrate good computational structure but contain **significant mathematical issues** that undermine the rigor of the claimed results. The most serious problems are:
18: 
19: 1. **Task 1.1:** Insufficient verification that all singularities are nodes (Hessian test incomplete)
20: 2. **Task 1.2:** T_Co is **constructed by fiat** rather than computed as orthogonal complement
21: 3. **Task 1.3:** Embeddings are **not rigorously constructed** — orthogonality claims unverified
22: 
23: ---
24: 
25: ## Detailed Analysis
26: 
27: ### 1. Task 1.1: Sextic Curve Construction (`task1_1_sextic.sage`)
28: 
29: **Claim:** Construct sextic F(x,y,z) = 0 with exactly 10 nodes.
30: 
31: #### ✓ Correct Elements:
32: 
33: - Parametrization approach is valid (generic degree-6 polynomials give rational sextic)
34: - Implicit equation via resultants is the correct method
35: - Affine chart z=1 plus line at infinity z=0 covers all of ℙ²
36: - Genus formula application is correct: g = g_a - Σδ = 10 - 10 = 0
37: 
38: #### ⚠️ **CRITICAL ISSUES:**
39: 
40: **Issue 1.1a: Hessian Test is Incomplete**
41: 
42: The script tests whether singularities are nodes by computing:
43: 
44: ```python
45: disc = F_xx(p) * F_yy(p) - F_xy(p)²
46: ```
47: 
48: **Problem:** This is the discriminant of the **affine** Hessian in the chart z=1. For a singularity to be a node (A₁), the **projective** Hessian must be nondegenerate.
49: 
50: **Mathematical Requirement:** A point p ∈ ℙ² is a node iff the Hessian matrix of second partial derivatives has rank 2 (not rank < 2). The full Hessian is the 3×3 matrix:
51: 
52: $$H(F)(p) = \begin{pmatrix} F_{xx} & F_{xy} & F_{xz} \\ F_{yx} & F_{yy} & F_{yz} \\ F_{zx} & F_{zy} & F_{zz} \end{pmatrix}$$
53: 
54: **The script only checks the 2×2 minor** `F_xx * F_yy - F_xy²`, which is insufficient. A singularity could have:
55: 
56: - degenerate affine Hessian (disc = 0) but still be a node in ℙ²
57: - nondegenerate affine Hessian but be a worse singularity (e.g., cusp)
58: 
59: **Correct Test:** Verify that the **full 3×3 Hessian has rank 2** at each singular point. For a homogeneous polynomial of degree d, at a singular point where F_x = F_y = F_z = 0, the Hessian criterion for an A₁ singularity is that the Hessian matrix has rank exactly 2 (one zero eigenvalue due to Euler relation, but no more).
60: 
61: **Issue 1.1b: No Verification of Irreducibility**
62: 
63: The script extracts "the sextic factor" but doesn't verify:
64: 
65: - The sextic is **irreducible** (could be union of lower-degree curves)
66: - The sextic is **reduced** (could have multiple components)
67: 
68: **Mathematical Requirement:** A Coble surface requires an **irreducible** rational sextic. If F factors, the geometric genus computation is invalid.
69: 
70: **Fix Required:**
71: 
72: ```python
73: # Add irreducibility check
74: assert F.is_irreducible(), "Sextic must be irreducible for Coble surface"
75: ```
76: 
77: **Issue 1.1c: No Multiplicity Check at Singularities**
78: 
79: The script assumes all singularities found are isolated nodes, but doesn't verify:
80: 
81: - Each singularity has **multiplicity exactly 2** (required for nodes)
82: - There are **no non-isolated singularities** (e.g., singular curves)
83: 
84: **Mathematical Requirement:** A node is an ordinary double point (multiplicity 2, two distinct tangent directions). The script should verify:
85: 
86: 1. mult_p(F) = 2 for each singular point p
87: 2. The tangent cone at p consists of two distinct lines
88: 
89: **Recommendation:** Use `F.multiplicity(p)` and verify the tangent cone factorization.
90: 
91: ---
92: 
93: ### 2. Task 1.2: Gram Matrices (`task1_2_gram_matrices.sage`)
94: 
95: **Claim:** Compute Gram matrices for S_Co and T_Co, verify invariants.
96: 
97: #### ✓ Correct Elements:
98: 
99: - S_Co Gram matrix `diag(2, -2, ..., -2)` is correct for Coble Picard lattice
100: - Signature computations are correct: S_Co has (1, 10), T_Co should have (2, 9)
101: - Discriminant group order 2¹¹ is correct for both lattices
102: - K3 lattice construction U³ ⊕ E₈(-1)² is correct
103: 
104: #### ⚠️ **CRITICAL ISSUES:**
105: 
106: **Issue 2.2a: T_Co is Constructed by Fiat, Not Computed**
107: 
108: **This is the most serious mathematical flaw in all three scripts.**
109: 
110: The script states:
111: 
112: ```python
113: # Construct T_Co with signature (2, 9) and discriminant (ℤ/2ℤ)^11
114: T_Co_gram = diagonal_matrix(QQ, [2, 2] + [-2]*9)
115: ```
116: 
117: **Problem:** T_Co is **defined** as S_Co^⊥ in Λ_K3, but the script never computes this orthogonal complement. Instead, it **asserts** that T_Co has a diagonal Gram matrix with the right signature and discriminant.
118: 
119: **Why This is Mathematically Invalid:**
120: 
121: 1. **No proof that S_Co embeds into Λ_K3:** The script assumes S_Co ↪ Λ_K3 exists but never constructs the embedding. By Nikulin's embedding theorem, such an embedding exists iff certain conditions hold, but these are never verified computationally.
122: 
123: 2. **No proof that the orthogonal complement has the claimed form:** Even if S_Co embeds, T_Co = S_Co^⊥ is determined by the embedding, not by fiat. Different embeddings can give non-isometric orthogonal complements.
124: 
125: 3. **The discriminant form relation q_T = -q_S is asserted, not verified:** For complementary lattices in a unimodular lattice, we must have (A_T, q_T) ≅ (A_S, -q_S). The script checks that both have order 2¹¹ but never verifies the quadratic forms match.
126: 
127: **What Should Be Done:**
128: 
129: ```python
130: # Step 1: Embed S_Co into Λ_K3 explicitly
131: # Step 2: Compute T_Co = S_Co^⊥ as kernel of pairing
132: # Step 3: Compute Gram matrix of T_Co from the embedding
133: # Step 4: Verify T_Co has correct invariants
134: # Step 5: Verify q_T ≅ -q_S on discriminant groups
135: ```
136: 
137: **Issue 2.2b: Primitivity Check is Circular**
138: 
139: The script checks:
140: 
141: ```python
142: if abs(S_Co.determinant()) == abs(T_Co.determinant()):
143:     print("Embedding is PRIMITIVE")
144: ```
145: 
146: **Problem:** This criterion is only valid **after** T_Co is computed as the orthogonal complement. Since T_Co is constructed independently, this "verification" is meaningless—it just checks that two diagonal matrices happen to have the same determinant (both are 2¹¹ by construction).
147: 
148: **Correct Approach:** Primitivity means Λ_K3 / S_Co is torsion-free. This should be verified by:
149: 
150: 1. Computing the saturation of S_Co in Λ_K3
151: 2. Checking that S_Co equals its saturation
152: 
153: **Issue 2.2c: Genus Uniqueness Argument is Incomplete**
154: 
155: The script correctly notes that r = a = 11 is a "boundary case" for Nikulin's classification, but the conclusion that the genus contains a unique class relies on signature mod 8 analysis that is **stated but not computed**.
156: 
157: **Mathematical Requirement:** For 2-elementary lattices with r = a and δ = 1, genus uniqueness depends on:
158: 
159: - The signature (p, q) mod 8
160: - Whether the lattice is of "type I" or "type II"
161: 
162: The script asserts uniqueness but doesn't compute the relevant invariants (e.g., the Brown invariant of the discriminant form).
163: 
164: ---
165: 
166: ### 3. Task 1.3: Embeddings (`task1_3_embeddings.sage`)
167: 
168: **Claim:** Construct explicit primitive embedding matrices T_Co → T_En → T_dP → Λ_K3.
169: 
170: #### ✓ Correct Elements:
171: 
172: - K3 lattice construction is correct
173: - Strategy of using U factors for orthogonal vectors is sound
174: - Simple root selection in E₈ (α₁, α₃, α₅, α₇ are mutually orthogonal) is correct
175: - Smith normal form test for primitivity is the right criterion
176: 
177: #### ⚠️ **CRITICAL ISSUES:**
178: 
179: **Issue 3.3a: S_Co Embedding is Not Verified to be Orthogonal**
180: 
181: The script constructs M_SCo_explicit and claims:
182: 
183: ```python
184: print(f"Matches S_Co_gram: {S_Co_check == S_Co_gram}")
185: ```
186: 
187: **Problem:** The script **abandons** the orthogonal embedding approach when it discovers that E₈ simple roots aren't all mutually orthogonal, then switches to a computational kernel method without verifying the result.
188: 
189: Looking at the actual construction:
190: 
191: ```python
192: # s_4 ↦ α_1 in E8_a (position 6)
193: # s_5 ↦ α_3 in E8_a (position 8)
194: # s_6 ↦ α_5 in E8_a (position 10)
195: # s_7 ↦ α_7 in E8_a (position 12)
196: ```
197: 
198: **Verification Required:** The script should print `S_Co_check.is_diagonal()` and verify it equals `S_Co_gram`. If the E₈ simple roots α₁, α₃, α₅, α₇ are truly orthogonal in the E₈(-1) metric, then `S_Co_check` should be diagonal with entries (2, -2, -2, ..., -2).
199: 
200: **But this is never actually checked in the output!** The script prints the check but doesn't halt if it fails.
201: 
202: **Issue 3.3b: T_Co Orthogonal Complement is Computed, But Not Verified**
203: 
204: In Section 6, the script computes:
205: 
206: ```python
207: pairing = M_SCo_explicit.transpose() * Lambda_K3
208: kernel_QQ = pairing_QQ.right_kernel()
209: ```
210: 
211: **Problem:** The computed T_Co basis vectors are **cleared of denominators** arbitrarily:
212: 
213: ```python
214: T_Co_basis = [v.denominator() * v for v in kernel_QQ.basis()]
215: ```
216: 
217: This produces an integer lattice, but:
218: 
219: 1. It may not be **primitive** in Λ_K3 (the scaling could introduce torsion)
220: 2. The resulting Gram matrix `T_Co_computed_gram` is never compared to the expected `diag(2, 2, -2, ..., -2)`
221: 3. The script never verifies that T_Co ⊕ S_Co = Λ_K3 (i.e., that they span the full lattice)
222: 
223: **Mathematical Requirement:** After computing the kernel, one must:
224: 
225: 1. Compute the saturation to ensure primitivity
226: 2. Verify the discriminant form relation q_T ≅ -q_S
227: 3. Check that the direct sum S_Co ⊕ T_Co has index 1 in Λ_K3
228: 
229: **Issue 3.3c: T_En and T_dP are Defined by Truncation, Not Geometry**
230: 
231: The script defines:
232: 
233: ```python
234: M_En_Co = matrix(ZZ, 11, 10, lambda i, j: 1 if i == j else 0)
235: M_dP_En = matrix(ZZ, 10, 9, lambda i, j: 1 if i == j else 0)
236: ```
237: 
238: **Problem:** These embeddings are **purely formal**—they just take the first 10 or 9 basis vectors of T_Co. There's no geometric justification for why:
239: 
240: - T_En should be a sublattice of T_Co
241: - T_dP should be a sublattice of T_En
242: - These specific sublattices have the correct geometric meaning for Enriques/del Pezzo surfaces
243: 
244: **Mathematical Requirement:** The embeddings T_Co → T_En → T_dP should come from:
245: 
246: 1. The geometry of the surfaces (e.g., T_En is the transcendental lattice of the Enriques surface obtained as a double cover)
247: 2. Explicit lattice-theoretic constructions (e.g., T_En is the invariant sublattice under an involution)
248: 
249: The script provides neither.
250: 
251: **Issue 3.3d: Primitivity Test is Applied to Identity Matrices**
252: 
253: The primitivity test:
254: 
255: ```python
256: print(f"T_En → T_Co primitive: {is_primitive_embedding(M_En_Co)}")
257: ```
258: 
259: **Problem:** `M_En_Co` is just a 11×10 matrix with 1s on the diagonal. This is **trivially primitive** because it's an inclusion of a direct summand. The test tells us nothing about whether the actual geometric embedding T_En ↪ T_Co is primitive.
260: 
261: **What Should Be Tested:** The primitivity of T_Co ↪ Λ_K3 (which is nontrivial), not the formal inclusions between abstract lattices.
262: 
263: ---
264: 
265: ## Summary of Required Fixes
266: 
267: ### Task 1.1 (Sextic Construction):
268: 
269: 1. **Replace Hessian test** with full 3×3 Hessian rank check
270: 2. **Add irreducibility verification** for the sextic polynomial
271: 3. **Verify multiplicity 2** at each singular point
272: 4. **Check tangent cone** has two distinct lines at each node
273: 
274: ### Task 1.2 (Gram Matrices):
275: 
276: 1. **Actually embed S_Co into Λ_K3** using Nikulin's embedding theorem
277: 2. **Compute T_Co as orthogonal complement**, not by fiat
278: 3. **Verify discriminant form relation** q_T ≅ -q_S explicitly
279: 4. **Check primitivity** via saturation, not just determinant comparison
280: 5. **Compute Brown invariant** to verify genus uniqueness in boundary case
281: 
282: ### Task 1.3 (Embeddings):
283: 
284: 1. **Verify S_Co embedding is orthogonal** (check S_Co_check == S_Co_gram)
285: 2. **Verify T_Co computed from kernel** has correct Gram matrix
286: 3. **Check S_Co ⊕ T_Co spans Λ_K3** (index 1)
287: 4. **Provide geometric justification** for T_En and T_dP sublattices
288: 5. **Remove trivial primitivity tests** on formal inclusions
289: 
290: ---
291: 
292: ## Conclusion
293: 
294: The scripts provide a **good computational framework** but the mathematical claims exceed what is actually verified. The most serious issue is **Task 1.2's construction of T_Co by fiat** rather than as a computed orthogonal complement. This undermines the entire lattice-theoretic foundation of the subsequent work.
295: 
296: **Recommendation:** Halt further computation until these foundational issues are resolved. The fixes require:
297: 
298: 1. Implementing proper orthogonal complement computation
299: 2. Adding rigorous singularity verification
300: 3. Providing geometric justification for lattice chains
301: 
302: **Estimated effort:** 2-3 days of focused work to correct the mathematical foundations.
303: 
304: ---
305: 
306: ## References
307: 
308: - Nikulin, V. V. (1979). "Integral symmetric bilinear forms and some of their geometric applications." Math. USSR Izvestija 14, 103-167.
309: - Conway, J. H. & Sloane, N. J. A. (1999). "Sphere Packings, Lattices and Groups." Springer, 3rd ed.
310: - Dolgachev, I. (2012). "Classical Algebraic Geometry: A Modern View." Cambridge University Press.

input.prompt:
We need to explicitly verify the discriminant form relation $q_T \cong -q_S \pmod{2\mathbb{Z}}$ for the Coble lattice $S_{Co}$ and its orthogonal complement $T_{Co}$ constructed in Task 1.2. 

In `task1_2_gram_matrices_fixed.sage`, the discriminant of $T_{Co}$ was computed to be 512 ($2^9$) instead of the expected 2048 ($2^{11}$) because a rational isometry $\Lambda_{K3}' = U^3 \oplus (-I_{16})$ was used for computational simplicity, instead of a primitive integral embedding into $U^3 \oplus E_8(-1)^2$.

Write a script `computations/task1_2b_discriminant_forms.sage` that:
1. Constructs $S_{Co}$ with Gram matrix $\text{diag}(2, -2, \dots, -2)$ (11×11).
2. Computes the discriminant group $A_S = S_{Co}^* / S_{Co}$ and its quadratic form $q_S$.
3. Constructs $T_{Co}$ integrally as a lattice with signature (2,9) and verify $r=a=11$. You can use $\langle 2 \rangle^2 \oplus \langle -2 \rangle^9$ and compute its discriminant form $q_{T_0}$.
4. Prove that $q_S \cong -q_{T_0} \pmod{2\mathbb{Z}}$. Note that for diagonal discriminant forms, two such forms are isomorphic if their Brown invariants match and they have the same invariants. Show this isomorphism computationally.
5. Create a more robust integral primitive embedding of $S_{Co}$ into $\Lambda_{K3} = U^3 \oplus E_8(-1)^2$ and properly compute the true $T_{Co}$ as the kernel over $\mathbb{Z}$, then compute its $q_T$ and compare.

Be mathematically rigorous in verifying the discriminant forms match.
